turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion in case 21-1170 Ciminelli v. United States.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit. Petitioner Louis Ciminelli was convicted of federal wire fraud and conspiracy to commit wire fraud for his role in the scheme to ensure that his construction company was awarded a contract from a New York Public Infrastructure Fund. Ciminelli's conviction was based on the Second Circuit's right to control theory of fraud. Under this theory, the government can establish wire fraud by showing that the defendant schemed to deprive a victim of potentially valuable economic information that is necessary to make discretionary economic decisions. The government charges theory in its indictment defeated a motion to dismiss based on that theory and relied on it in its summation to the jury. The District Court followed Second Circuit precedent and instructed the jury on the right to control theory. The Second Circuit affirmed Ciminelli's conviction based on its long-standing precedents endorsing that theory. In an opinion filed with the Court today, we reverse the Wire Fraud Statute 18 U.S.C. �1343 criminalizes ?schemes or artifices to defraud or for obtaining money or property by means of false or fraudulent pretenses representations or promises?. We have long haul that this statute reaches only interests that had been recognized as property when the Wire Fraud Statute was enacted. The right to potentially valuable economic information necessary to dispose off assets was not a traditionally recognized property interest when the Wire Fraud Statute was enacted. Accordingly the Second Circuit's right to control theory cannot form the basis for a wire fraud conviction. Although the government indicted obtained the conviction and prevailed on appeal based solely on the right to control theory, the government now concedes that the theory was as articulated by the Second Circuit is erroneous. We decline to reach the government's alternative theory of liability however because the government raises it for the first time before this Court and relied solely on the right to control theory below. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we reversed the judgment of the Second Circuit. The opinion of the Court is unanimous. Justice Alito has filed a concurring opinion.
